topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered open, and there it was—a thick, cream-colored card, entirely blank save for a hastily scrawled, looping script that read, "Happy Birthday." My fingers, already tense from a long day, froze mid-reach. I pulled it out, the paper feeling strangely slick under my touch. A wave of profound unpleasantness washed over me, making the air in the hallway feel thick and heavy. Who would send this? And why, on an occasion meant for celebration, this utter void? I crumpled the card slowly, the sound of the tearing paper loud in the quiet apartment. I shoved it deep into the recycling bin, wanting nothing more than to erase the sight of it from my immediate vicinity.

[story 2]

I picked up the envelope, my knuckles white against the cardboard. Inside, the card was pristine, the message a cheerful, saccharine declaration of birthday wishes. As I unfolded it, a sharp, involuntary shudder ran through me. It was too perfect. The handwriting was too practiced, the sentiment utterly hollow. I stared at the looping cursive, my jaw tightening until it ached. I dropped it onto the floor, the sound echoing sharply. I didn't bother picking it up again; I just stood there, breathing in shallow gasps, my gaze fixed on the offending piece of paper as if it were contaminated.

[story 3]

The familiar rustle of the mail was usually a mundane event, but today, something snagged my attention. A card, unaddressed, lay nestled in the pile. My breath hitched. I picked it up, the texture of the cardstock feeling strangely cheap against my palm. When I read the simple greeting, a cold knot formed in my stomach, tightening around my ribs. I felt an immediate, visceral recoil. I didn't want to look at it again. With a jerky movement, I swept the card off the surface and tossed it carelessly toward the wastebasket, the motion fueled by a sudden, intense need to cleanse my space of whatever it represented.
